name: Dockerize and Deploy a React/Next.js App
description: Create a production-optimized Docker image for a Next.js application with multi-stage builds and minimal image size.

steps.title: Create a multi-stage Dockerfile
steps.description: Write a Dockerfile that uses separate stages for dependencies, building, and the final runtime image.
steps.command:
cat > Dockerfile << 'EOF'
FROM node:20-alpine AS base

FROM base AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

FROM base AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN corepack enable pnpm && pnpm build

FROM base AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
EOF
steps.tip: Enable standalone output in next.config.ts: output: 'standalone' for a minimal production server.

steps.title: Add a .dockerignore file
steps.description: Exclude unnecessary files from the Docker build context to speed up builds.

steps.title: Enable standalone output in Next.js config
steps.description: Configure Next.js to produce a self-contained output that does not need node_modules.
steps.warning: Without standalone output, the final Docker image will include all of node_modules and be significantly larger.

steps.title: Build the Docker image
steps.description: Build the production image with a descriptive tag.

steps.title: Run the container
steps.description: Start the container and map port 3000.
steps.tip: The standalone image is typically 100-150MB compared to 1GB+ without multi-stage builds.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Dockerize and Deploy Next.js App
set -euo pipefail

APP_NAME=${1:-my-nextjs-app}
TAG=${2:-latest}

echo "=== Building Docker image ==="
docker build -t "$APP_NAME:$TAG" .

echo ""
echo "=== Image size ==="
docker images "$APP_NAME:$TAG" --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}} - {{.Size}}"

echo ""
echo "=== Running container ==="
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --name "$APP_NAME" "$APP_NAME:$TAG"

echo ""
echo "=== Container status ==="
docker ps --filter "name=$APP_NAME" --format "table {{.Names}}	{{.Status}}	{{.Ports}}"

echo ""
echo "App running at http://localhost:3000"

faq.question: Why use multi-stage Docker builds?
faq.answer: Multi-stage builds separate the build environment from the runtime environment. The final image only contains the built output and runtime dependencies, reducing image size from 1GB+ to ~150MB.

faq.question: How do I pass environment variables to the container?
faq.answer: Use --env-file .env.production at runtime for secrets, or set build-time variables with ARG in the Dockerfile. Never bake secrets into the Docker image.

faq.question: Can I deploy this to cloud platforms?
faq.answer: Yes. Push the image to a registry (Docker Hub, ECR, GCR) and deploy to AWS ECS, Google Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps, or any Kubernetes cluster.
